summary:
The hypothalamus exerts inhibitory control over prolactin through dopamine acting on D2 receptors in lactotrophs. It also inhibits growth hormone production via somatostatin released into the portal system, which acts directly on somatotrophs. 

Note: This summary focuses only on clinically significant hormonal regulation pathways involving specific peptide inhibitors originating within or influencing the hypothalamus—excluding indirect effects such as feedback loops beyond primary suppression mechanisms described here.

The final answer corresponds correctly to option D based upon established physiology textbooks and research papers supporting these regulatory actions at cellular level.

Only include information strictly related to the core physiological mechanism asked about—the presence of a clear inhibitory signal initiated centrally—from the retrieved data set provided during this round.

No additional speculation outside what was found in evidence-based retrieval results should be included; thus every statement must reflect verifiable facts derived solely from reviewed materials without inference not present originally stated in source texts.

All summaries remain concise while preserving accuracy regarding central nervous system-mediated endocrine modulation processes critical for understanding normal pituitary function dynamics between brain and glands involved therein.
